food_subgroup: Fermented vegetables — salt-fermented shredded white cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata), naturally fermented by lactic acid bacteria

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sauerkraut as a food product in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard condiment serving of sauerkraut is approximately 50g (3–4 tablespoons), providing about 10 kcal, 2g carbohydrate, 1.5g dietary fibre, and approximately 330mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Sauerkraut, canned — USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169227
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC 169227 is the canonical approved-source entry for sauerkraut (canned/commercial packaged). Values are directly sourced from this entry. This record covers both pasteurised (canned/jarred) and unpasteurised (refrigerated) commercial sauerkraut; nutrient macros are equivalent between forms; probiotic content differs (pasteurised = no live bacteria; unpasteurised = L. plantarum 10^7–10^8 CFU/g).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fermented vegetables — analogous estimate based on low digestible carbohydrate content and high dietary fibre; consistent with published GI values for fermented/pickled low-carbohydrate vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate) / peer-reviewed literature on fermented vegetable GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for sauerkraut is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~15 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) very low digestible carbohydrate content (4.3g/100g total carbohydrate, of which 2.9g is dietary fibre, leaving ~1.4g net digestible carbohydrate per 100g); (2) high lactic acid content from fermentation, which further blunts glycaemic response; (3) published GI values for similar fermented/pickled low-carbohydrate vegetables in peer-reviewed literature (GI range 10–20). GL per 50g serving = (15 × 2.15g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.3 (negligible).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium density — 661mg sodium per 100g; approximately 330mg per standard 50g condiment serving
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should rinse canned or jarred sauerkraut under cold running water for 30 seconds before serving, which reduces sodium content by approximately 30–40% (to ~220–230mg per 50g serving), and should limit portion size to 30g or less as a condiment; unpasteurised sauerkraut from refrigerated packs carries the same sodium burden and should be treated identically.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycaemic impact — GI ~15, GL ~0.3 per 50g serving; high dietary fibre (2.9g/100g) slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sauerkraut is well-suited for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load (GL ~0.3 per 50g serving), high dietary fibre content, and evidence that L. plantarum from unpasteurised sauerkraut may modestly support glucose metabolism; sodium content should still be monitored if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high fibre are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, sauerkraut's high sodium content (661mg/100g) is the primary concern and warrants rinsing before consumption and limiting serving size to 30g; the food's zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (2.9g/100g), and vitamin C (20mg/100g) are cardioprotective attributes that support its inclusion in small condiment quantities within a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory glucosinolate-derived isothiocyanates preserved through fermentation; gut microbiome support from L. plantarum in unpasteurised forms
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unpasteurised sauerkraut providing L. plantarum (10^7–10^8 CFU/g) may support gut microbiome diversity with indirect anti-inflammatory benefit; glucosinolate-derived isothiocyanates from cabbage are preserved during fermentation and have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in vitro; sauerkraut is very low in calories and fat, making it suitable as a condiment for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauerkraut is a traditional Central European fermented food made from finely shredded white cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata) preserved through lacto-fermentation with salt. The fermentation process — driven by naturally occurring lactic acid bacteria, principally Lactobacillus plantarum — produces lactic acid, which gives sauerkraut its characteristic sharp, sour, tangy flavour and soft-crisp texture. Sauerkraut is sold commercially in two forms: pasteurised (canned or jarred, shelf-stable, no live bacteria) and unpasteurised (refrigerated, containing live L. plantarum at 10^7–10^8 CFU/g with probiotic potential). Per 100g, sauerkraut provides approximately 19 kcal, 0.9g protein, 0.1g fat, 4.3g carbohydrate (of which 2.9g is dietary fibre and 1.8g sugar), and 661mg sodium. It is an excellent source of vitamin C (20mg/100g) and folate (24mcg/100g). Glycaemic index is approximately 15 (low), with a negligible glycaemic load of ~0.3 per standard 50g condiment serving. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (approximately 330mg per 50g serving), which is significant for hypertensive and cardiovascular disease patients; rinsing before serving reduces sodium by 30–40%. Sauerkraut is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is widely available in Singapore at Cold Storage (Jardin Bio, Hengstenberg brands) and NTUC FairPrice. Historically, sauerkraut was used by Captain James Cook to prevent scurvy on Pacific voyages (1768–1780) due to its preserved vitamin C content. In Singapore, it is used as a condiment with hot dogs, bratwurst, and in German restaurant dishes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 169227, an approved primary source with full macro and micronutrient coverage. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with the food's negligible digestible carbohydrate content and published fermented vegetable GI literature. Ontology anchors (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are confirmed direct matches.
notes: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FDC 169227 — sauerkraut contains no saturated fat by composition. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FDC 169227. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant-derived food contains no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — plant-derived fermented food; no animal-derived B12 source. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FDC 169227. monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed monounsaturated fatty acid fraction is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 169227 for sauerkraut; total fat is only 0.1g/100g making this fraction nutritionally negligible and not available as a discrete value in the consulted approved source. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed polyunsaturated fatty acid fraction is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 169227 for sauerkraut; total fat is only 0.1g/100g making this fraction nutritionally negligible and not available as a discrete value in the consulted approved source. water_g set to -1: moisture content is not separately reported as a discrete nutrient value in USDA SR Legacy FDC 169227 for this sauerkraut entry; while sauerkraut is approximately 92% water by typical composition, this specific value is not confirmed from the consulted approved source (USDA FDC 169227) and is therefore not populated to maintain source integrity. GI note: No direct GI test for sauerkraut exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI ~15 is an analogous estimate based on negligible digestible carbohydrate (~1.4g net per 100g), high lactic acid content, and published GI ranges for comparable fermented low-carbohydrate vegetables. Probiotic note: pasteurised (canned/jarred) sauerkraut contains no live bacteria; only refrigerated unpasteurised sauerkraut provides L. plantarum probiotic benefit. Sodium management: rinsing sauerkraut under cold water for 30 seconds before serving reduces sodium by approximately 30–40%. Record covers commercial packaged sauerkraut available in Singapore (Jardin Bio / Organic brand at Cold Storage; Hengstenberg brand at NTUC FairPrice).
